elements.sent-2411-001: The control of legged robots, particularly humanoid and quadruped robots, presents significant challenges due to their high-dimensional and nonlinear dynamics.
elements.sent-2411-002: While linear systems can be effectively controlled using methods like Model Predictive Control (MPC), the control of nonlinear systems remains complex.
elements.sent-2411-003: One promising solution is the Koopman Operator, which approximates nonlinear dynamics with a linear model, enabling the use of proven linear control techniques.
elements.sent-2411-004: However, achieving accurate linearization through data-driven methods is difficult due to issues like approximation error, domain shifts, and the limitations of fixed linear state-space representations.
elements.sent-2411-005: These challenges restrict the scalability of Koopman-based approaches.
elements.sent-2411-006: This paper addresses these challenges by proposing a continual learning algorithm designed to iteratively refine Koopman dynamics for high-dimensional legged robots.
elements.sent-2411-007: The key idea is to progressively expand the dataset and latent space dimension, enabling the learned Koopman dynamics to converge towards accurate approximations of the true system dynamics.
elements.sent-2411-008: Theoretical analysis shows that the linear approximation error of our method converges monotonically.
elements.sent-2411-009: Experimental results demonstrate that our method achieves high control performance on robots like Unitree G1/H1/A1/Go2 and ANYmal D, across various terrains using simple linear MPC controllers.
elements.sent-2411-010: This work is the first to successfully apply linearized Koopman dynamics for locomotion control of high-dimensional legged robots, enabling a scalable model-based control solution.
elements.sent-2411-011: The control of legged robots has garnered increasing attention due to the growing potential of humanoid and quadruped robots in real-world applications.
elements.sent-2411-012: However, the task is challenging and is complicated by the high-dimensional and nonlinear dynamics inherent in legged systems.
elements.sent-2411-013: Existing methods for controlling nonlinear systems exhibit a trade-off between emphasizing controller synthesis and emphasizing modeling, and can be categorized into model-free and model-based approaches.
elements.sent-2411-014: Model-free methods directly derive the control law without explicitly modeling the system dynamics.
elements.sent-2411-015: Reinforcement learning (RL) is the representative example, demonstrating notable success in controlling legged robots in real-world applications \cite{gmm2021song,haarnoja2018soft, zhaoabsolute, he2024learning, lee2020learning, he2024learning, fu2024humanplushumanoidshadowingimitation, luo2023perpetual .}
elements.sent-2411-016: Despite their strong performance, model-free control laws are often highly task-specific and require retraining or fine-tuning when adapting to new capabilities.
elements.sent-2411-017: In contrast, model-based approaches decouple the modeling of system dynamics from the realization of task objectives, enabling more efficient adaptation to new tasks.
elements.sent-2411-018: The models used in these approaches can be catagorized as: original nonlinear models, locally linearized nonlinear models, globally linearized nonlinear models, with increasing modeling complexity and decreasing control complexity.
elements.sent-2411-019: For direct control of nonlinear models, \cite{yi2024covompctheoreticalanalysissamplingbased is available for controlling legged system with sampleing-based MPC, while \cite{Kazemi_Majd_Moghaddam_2013 applies robust backstepping control to quadruped robots.}}
elements.sent-2411-020: Local linearization methods, iLQR \cite{li2004iterative and NMPC \cite{grune2017nonlinear often combine analytical \cite{sotaro2023analytical,zhu2024convergentilqrsafetrajectory or learned models \cite{nagabandi2017neuralnetworkdynamicsmodelbased, liu2023modelbasedcontrolsparseneural with complex controllers.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-021: A promising alternative is employing Koopman Operator Theory to construct globally linearized nonlinear models of legged systems and enabling the use of simpler controller, like MPC, with performance guarantees.
elements.sent-2411-022: Although Koopman-based methods have proven effective in legged robot tasks such as motion planning \cite{kim2024learning and gait prediction \cite{KROLICKI2022420 , no attempts have been made to directly control legged systems.}}
elements.sent-2411-023: When deriving the Koopman Operator analytically is ineffective, our method leverages a data-driven approach that minimizes the need for expert knowledge while ensuring strong generalizability.
elements.sent-2411-024: Within data-driven approach, recent methods have applied Koopman dynamics to high-level motion planning \cite{kim2024learning, lyu2023task .}
elements.sent-2411-025: However, these methods still rely on end-to-end optimization toward task objectives, limiting the generalizability.
elements.sent-2411-026: In contrast, \cite{shi2022deep, korda2018linear,mamakoukas2021derivative focus on obtaining accurate Koopman approximations for low-level control, but their applicability is limited to low-dimensional systems \cite{shi2024koopmanoperatorsrobotlearning .}}
elements.sent-2411-027: Controlling high-dimensional nonlinear systems with Koopman is challenging due to (a) imperfect approximations and (b) domain shifts from limited state transition data that only spans a subspace of the full state space.
elements.sent-2411-028: Approximation errors make the system sensitive to poorly-modeled dynamics, while domain shifts cause failures under perturbations.
elements.sent-2411-029: To address the challenges, we propose a continual learning algorithm designed to progressively learn Koopman dynamics for high-dimensional legged robots.
elements.sent-2411-030: The central idea is to iteratively expand the dataset effectively while increasing the latent space dimension to accommodate the growing dataset.
elements.sent-2411-031: Upon convergence, the accumulated dataset spans a state space naturally within which the learned Koopman dynamics exhibit high control performance.
elements.sent-2411-032: Additionally, we provide a theoretical analysis of the convergence of the learned Koopman dynamics towards the true dynamics, validating the effectiveness of our approach.
elements.sent-2411-033: Experimental results demonstrate that our Koopman dynamics minimize linearization error after only a few iterations and enable effective locomotion control for legged robots using simple Model Predictive Control (MPC) controllers.
elements.sent-2411-035: An iterative continual learning algorithm designed to mitigate approximation errors and domain shifts, achieving high approximation accuracy in just a few iterations, with a theoretical guarantee of error convergence.
elements.sent-2411-036: This is the first work that achieves effective locomotion control for high-dimensional legged robots using linearized system dynamics with simple MPC controllers, with validation on the most advanced legged robots across various terrains.
elements.sent-2411-037: Koopman Operator Theory offers a powerful framework for analyzing nonlinear dynamical systems by mapping them into equivalent linear systems in an infinite-dimensional latent space.

elements.sent-2411-143: Nonlinear dynamic systems are generally controlled using two main approaches: model-free and model-based methods.
elements.sent-2411-144: Model-free methods, often associated with learning-based approaches, have become popular for addressing complex, high-dimensional systems in a data-driven manner.
elements.sent-2411-145: Reinforcement learning (RL) techniques, for instance, approximate a combination of system dynamics and control policies by training neural networks using methods like policy gradients or value iteration.
elements.sent-2411-146: Off-policy algorithms such as SAC \cite{haarnoja2018soft efficiently reuse data to learn control policies, whereas on-policy methods like PPO \cite{schulman2017proximal and its extension APO \cite{zhaoabsolute ensure monotonic policy improvement.}}}
elements.sent-2411-147: Another learning paradigm, imitation learning \cite{luo2023perpetual, he2024learning , leverages large expert datasets to guide policy development.}
elements.sent-2411-148: Despite their successes, these methods often suffer from challenges such as sample inefficiency, high computational cost, and limited generalization.
elements.sent-2411-149: Model-based methods, on the other hand, rely on leveraging system physics to construct accurate models.
elements.sent-2411-150: Techniques like iLQR \cite{li2004iterative and NMPC \cite{grune2017nonlinear, liu2023modelbasedcontrolsparseneural use these models to optimize control trajectories, while methods such as backstepping \cite{fossen1998nonlinear are well-suited for systems with well-defined dynamics.}}}
elements.sent-2411-151: Neural network-based approaches, such as \cite{nagabandi2017neuralnetworkdynamicsmodelbased , extend these ideas by approximating nonlinear dynamics for control tasks.}
elements.sent-2411-152: Moreover, model-based RL combines data-driven techniques with physical modeling to describe nonlinear system dynamics.
elements.sent-2411-153: For instance, \cite{gmm2021song uses RL workflows to fit multiple local approximations of global dynamics, while \cite{nagabandi2017neuralnetworkdynamicsmodelbased employs RL reward functions to learn dynamics from extensive random-shooting data.}}
elements.sent-2411-154: However, even these approaches face significant challenges when applied to high-dimensional systems, such as legged robots, where designing accurate models becomes increasingly complex and task-specific.. Koopman Operator Theory.
elements.sent-2411-155: While model-based methods struggle with complex, high-dimensional systems and model-free methods lack interpretability and flexibility, linearizing nonlinear dynamics has recently emerged as a promising approach.
elements.sent-2411-156: Among these, Koopman Operator Theory has garnered attention for its ability to represent nonlinear dynamics in a higher-dimensional linear space.
elements.sent-2411-157: Current research on Koopman theory in control can be categorized into three main directions: (i) Koopman Operator as a Feature Encoder: The Koopman operator has been applied in some methods to predict future states based on the current state \cite{han2023utility or vision inputs \cite{chen2024korol .}}
elements.sent-2411-158: However, these approaches primarily serve as feature extractors and do not fully exploit the benefits of linearization.
elements.sent-2411-159: Additionally, designing an effective Koopman operator is often challenging due to the requirement for infinite-dimensional representations and the inefficiency of available data \cite{kaiser2020data .}
elements.sent-2411-160: While this integration aims to benefit from Koopman’s properties, it still inherits the challenges of learning-based methods, particularly in terms of interpretability and generalization.
elements.sent-2411-161: For example, \cite{shi2022deep applies LQR to a deep-learned Koopman operator, while others use radial basis functions (RBF) \cite{korda2018linear or derivative basis functions \cite{mamakoukas2021derivative to design the operator.}}}
elements.sent-2411-162: Despite these advances, current methods struggle to control high-dimensional systems, like legged robots due to severe model mismatch and domain shifts.

elements.algorithm-2411-001: \caption{Incremental Koopman Algorithm }\label{alg:koopman} \begin{algorithmic} \STATE Input: MPC Controller $\pi_{mpc}$, Initial latent space dimension $n^{(0)}$, Initial dynamics $\mathcal{T}^{(0)}=(g^{(0)}_{n^{(0)}}, A^{(0)}_{n^{(0)}}, B^{(0)}_{n^{(0)}})$, Initial dataset $\mathcal{D}^{(0)}$, Reference repository $\mathcal{R}$, Dimension increment step size $\Delta n$ and Initial training epochs $J^{(0)}$ \STATE ~~~ \STATE Function: TrainKoopman($n, \mathcal{D}, J$): \STATE $optimizaer \leftarrow$ Adam(); \STATE $scheduler \leftarrow$ CosineAnnealingLR(); \STATE $\mathcal{T} \doteq (g_n, A_n, B_n);$ \COMMENT{\textcolor{blue}{Initialization of Koopman dynamics}} \FOR{$k=0,1,2,\dots,J-1$} \FOR{$\mathcal{B}$ in $\mathcal{D}$} \STATE $\mathcal{B}_z \leftarrow g(\mathcal{B});$ \COMMENT{\textcolor{blue}{Embed $x \in \mathcal{B}$ into latent vectors $z$}} \STATE $l_k \leftarrow \mathcal{L}_{koopman}(\mathcal{T}, \mathcal{B}, \mathcal{B}_z);$ \COMMENT{\textcolor{blue}{Refer to \Cref{eq: loss} for $\mathcal{L}_{koopman}$}} \STATE $optimizer$.zero\_grad(); \STATE $l_k$.backward(); \STATE $optimizer$.step(); \ENDFOR \STATE $scheduler.step();$ \ENDFOR \STATE $return~ \mathcal{T};$ \STATE ~~~ \FOR{$j=0,1,2,\dots$} \STATE $n^{(j+1)} \leftarrow n^{(j)} + \Delta n;$ \COMMENT{\textcolor{blue}{Increase latent space dimension}} \STATE $\mathcal{D}^{(j+1)}_{incre} \leftarrow \pi_{mpc}(\mathcal{T}^{(j)}, \mathcal{R});$ \STATE $\mathcal{D}^{(j+1)} \leftarrow \{\mathcal{D}^{(j)}, \mathcal{D}^{(j+1)}_{incre}\};$ \COMMENT{\textcolor{blue}{Dataset augmentation}} \STATE $J^{(j+1)} \leftarrow J^{(j)};$ \STATE $\mathcal{T}^{(j+1)} \leftarrow$ TrainKoopman($n^{(j+1)}, \mathcal{D}^{(j+1)}, J^{(j+1)}$); \WHILE{TrainKoopman failed} \STATE $J^{(j+1)} \leftarrow \frac{J^{(j+1)}}{2};$ \COMMENT{\textcolor{blue}{Adjust epochs when collapsing}} \STATE $\mathcal{T}^{(j+1)} \leftarrow$ TrainKoopman($n^{(j+1)}, \mathcal{D}^{(j+1)}, J^{(j+1)}$); \ENDWHILE \IF{TrackConverge$(\mathcal{T}^{(j+1)}, \mathcal{T}^{(j)})$} \STATE $Break;$ \COMMENT{\textcolor{blue}{Quit if tracking performance converge}} \ENDIF \ENDFOR \end{algorithmic}

elements.algorithm-2411-002: [H] \caption{Weighted Tanimoto ELM.}\label{alg:alg1} \begin{algorithmic} \STATE \STATE {TRAIN}$(\mathbf{X} \mathbf{T})$ \STATE $ select randomly W \subset \mathbf{X} $ \STATE $ N_\mathbf{t} \gets | \{ i : \mathbf{t}_i = \mathbf{t} \} | $ for $ \mathbf{t}= -1,+1 $ \STATE $ B_i \gets \sqrt{ max(N_{-1},N_{+1}) / N_{\mathbf{t}_i} } $ for $ i = 1,...,N $ \STATE $ \hat{\mathbf{H}} \gets B \cdot (\mathbf{X}^TW)/( \mathbb{1}\mathbf{X} + \mathbb{1}W - \mathbf{X}^TW ) $ \STATE $ \beta \gets \left ( I/C + \hat{\mathbf{H}}^T\hat{\mathbf{H}} \right )^{-1}(\hat{\mathbf{H}}^T B\cdot \mathbf{T}) $ \STATE return $W, \beta $ \STATE \STATE {PREDICT}$(\mathbf{X} )$ \STATE $ \mathbf{H} \gets (\mathbf{X}^TW )/( \mathbb{1}\mathbf{X} + \mathbb{1}W- \mathbf{X}^TW ) $ \STATE return $sign( \mathbf{H} \beta )$ \end{algorithmic} \label{alg1}

elements.sent-2411-415: This paper addresses these challenges by proposing Incremental Koopman algorithm designed to iteratively refine Koopman dynamics for high-dimensional legged robots.
elements.sent-2411-416: Experimental results demonstrate that our method achieves high control performance on robots like Unitree G1, H1, A1, Go2 and ANYmal D, across various terrains using simple linear MPC controllers.
elements.sent-2411-417: However, the task is complicated with challenges due to the high-dimensional and nonlinear dynamics inherent in legged systems.
elements.sent-2411-418: Existing control methods for nonlinear systems exhibit a trade-off between controller synthesis and modeling accuracy, falling into model-free and model-based approaches.
elements.sent-2411-419: Reinforcement learning (RL) is the representative example, demonstrating notable success in controlling legged robots in real-world applications \cite{gmm2021song,haarnoja2018soft, zhaoabsolute, lee2020learning, he2024learning, fu2024humanplushumanoidshadowingimitation, luo2023perpetual .}
elements.sent-2411-420: Despite their strong performance, model-free control laws are often highly task-specific and require retraining or fine-tuning when adapting to new tasks.
elements.sent-2411-421: Model-based approaches decouple the modeling of system dynamics from the realization of task objectives, enabling more efficient adaptation to new tasks.
elements.sent-2411-422: The models used in these approaches can be catagorized as: original nonlinear models, locally linearized models, and globally linearized models, with increasing modeling complexity and decreasing control complexity.
elements.sent-2411-423: For direct control of nonlinear models, \cite{yi2024covompctheoreticalanalysissamplingbased introduced sampling-based MPC for legged robots, while \cite{Kazemi_Majd_Moghaddam_2013 applied robust backstepping control to quadruped robots.}}
elements.sent-2411-424: Local linearization methods, iLQR and NMPC often combine analytical \cite{sotaro2023analytical,zhu2024convergentilqrsafetrajectory,zhang2024robotsattitudesingularityfreequaternionbased or learned models \cite{nagabandi2017neuralnetworkdynamicsmodelbased, liu2023modelbasedcontrolsparseneural with complex controllers.}}
elements.sent-2411-425: A promising alternative to these approaches is employing Koopman Operator Theory to construct globally linearized models of legged systems and enabling the use of a simpler controller, e.g., linear MPC, with performance guarantees.
elements.sent-2411-426: Although Koopman-based methods have proven effective in motion planning \cite{kim2024learning and gait prediction \cite{KROLICKI2022420 , no prior work has directly modeled the whole body dynamics of legged robots with Koopman Operator.}}
elements.sent-2411-427: A Koopman Operator can be derived analytically or approximated through data-driven approaches.
elements.sent-2411-428: While analytical approaches are extremely challenging for high-dimensional nonlinear systems like legged robots, our method adopts a data-driven approach that reduces reliance on expert knowledge and enhances generalizability.
elements.sent-2411-429: Recent works have applied data-driven approaches to obtain linear models for high-level motion planning \cite{kim2024learning, lyu2023task .}
elements.sent-2411-430: However, these methods typically depend on end-to-end optimization aimed at specific task objectives, which can limit their broader applicability.
elements.sent-2411-431: In contrast, \cite{shi2022deep, korda2018linear,mamakoukas2021derivative have focused on obtaining accurate Koopman approximations for low-level control, but their applicability is limited to low-dimensional systems \cite{shi2024koopmanoperatorsrobotlearning .}}
elements.sent-2411-432: It remains challenging to apply Koopman Operator to linearize high-dimensional nonlinear systems due to (a) imperfect approximations and (b) domain shifts arising from limited state transition data that only covers a subspace of the entire state space.
elements.sent-2411-433: Such approximation errors make the system sensitive to inaccurately modeled dynamics, while domain shifts cause failures under perturbations.
elements.sent-2411-434: To address these challenges, we propose an incremental Koopman algorithm tailored for high-dimensional legged robots, designed to continually learn and lift Koopman dynamics.
elements.sent-2411-435: The core strategy involves progressively expanding the training dataset and the dimension of the latent space.
elements.sent-2411-436: As the dataset and latent space grow, it naturally spans a state space where the refined Koopman dynamics achieve minimized linear approximation errors upon convergence.
elements.sent-2411-437: Additionally, we provide a theoretical analysis to validate the convergence of the learned Koopman dynamics towards the true Koopman dynamics.
elements.sent-2411-438: Experimental results demonstrate that our approach reduces linearization error after only a few iterations and enables effective locomotion control for legged robots using Model Predictive Control (MPC).
elements.sent-2411-439: Our key contributions are summarized as follows:.
elements.sent-2411-440: We propose an iterative continual learning algorithm to mitigate linear approximation errors for Koopman dynamics, supported by a theoretical guarantee of convergence.
elements.sent-2411-441: Our approach is the first work to successfully implement locomotion control for high-dimensional legged robots using linearized system dynamics with linear MPC controllers.

elements.algorithm-2411-003: \caption{Incremental Koopman Algorithm }\label{alg:koopman} \begin{algorithmic} \STATE Input: MPC Controller $\pi_{mpc}$, Initial latent space dimension $n^{(0)}$, Initial dynamics $\mathcal{T}^{(0)}=(g^{(0)}_{n^{(0)}}, A^{(0)}_{n^{(0)}}, B^{(0)}_{n^{(0)}})$, Initial dataset $\mathcal{D}^{(0)}$, Reference repository $\mathcal{R}$, Dimension increment step size $\Delta n$ and Initial training epochs $J^{(0)}$ \STATE ~~~ \STATE function: TrainKoopman($n, \mathcal{D}, J$): \begin{ALC@g} \STATE $optimizaer \leftarrow$ Adam(); \STATE $scheduler \leftarrow$ CosineAnnealingLR(); \STATE $\mathcal{T} \doteq (g_n, A_n, B_n);$ \COMMENT{\textcolor{blue}{Initialization of Koopman dynamics}} \FOR{$k=0,1,2,\dots,J-1$} \FOR{$\mathcal{B}$ in $\mathcal{D}$} \STATE $\mathcal{B}_z \leftarrow g(\mathcal{B});$ \COMMENT{\textcolor{blue}{Embed $x \in \mathcal{B}$ into latent vectors $z$}} \STATE $l_k \leftarrow \mathcal{L}_{koopman}(\mathcal{T}, \mathcal{B}, \mathcal{B}_z);$ \COMMENT{\textcolor{blue}{Refer to \Cref{eq: loss} for $\mathcal{L}_{koopman}$}} \STATE $optimizer$.zero\_grad(); \STATE $l_k$.backward(); \STATE $optimizer$.step(); \ENDFOR \STATE $scheduler.step();$ \ENDFOR \STATE $return~ \mathcal{T};$ \end{ALC@g} \STATE end function \STATE ~~~ \FOR{$j=0,1,2,\dots$} \STATE $n^{(j+1)} \leftarrow n^{(j)} + \Delta n;$ \COMMENT{\textcolor{blue}{Increase latent space dimension}} \STATE $\mathcal{D}^{(j+1)}_{incre} \leftarrow \pi_{mpc}(\mathcal{T}^{(j)}, \mathcal{R});$ \STATE $\mathcal{D}^{(j+1)} \leftarrow \{\mathcal{D}^{(j)}, \mathcal{D}^{(j+1)}_{incre}\};$ \COMMENT{\textcolor{blue}{Dataset augmentation}} \STATE $J^{(j+1)} \leftarrow J^{(j)};$ \STATE $\mathcal{T}^{(j+1)} \leftarrow$ TrainKoopman($n^{(j+1)}, \mathcal{D}^{(j+1)}, J^{(j+1)}$); \WHILE{TrainKoopman failed} \STATE $J^{(j+1)} \leftarrow \frac{J^{(j+1)}}{2};$ \COMMENT{\textcolor{blue}{Adjust epochs when collapsing}} \STATE $\mathcal{T}^{(j+1)} \leftarrow$ TrainKoopman($n^{(j+1)}, \mathcal{D}^{(j+1)}, J^{(j+1)}$); \ENDWHILE \IF{TrackConverge$(\mathcal{T}^{(j+1)}, \mathcal{T}^{(j)})$} \STATE $Break;$ \COMMENT{\textcolor{blue}{Quit if tracking performance converge}} \ENDIF \ENDFOR \end{algorithmic}

elements.sent-2411-513: Koopman Operator Theory offers a powerful framework for analyzing nonlinear dynamical systems by representing them through infinite-dimensional linear operators acting on observable functions.
elements.sent-2411-514: Extended Dynamic Mode Decomposition (EDMD) is a popular method that approximates the Koopman operator within a finite-dimensional subspace spanned by a chosen set of observables.
elements.sent-2411-515: Recent advancements incorporate neural networks to learn these observables adaptively, enhancing the approximation's flexibility and expressiveness.. Definitions:.
elements.sent-2411-516: Hilbert Space \( \mathcal{H \): Let \( \mathcal{H \) be a separable Hilbert space of observables, equipped with an inner product \( \langle \cdot, \cdot \rangle \) and corresponding norm \( \| \cdot \| \).}}
elements.sent-2411-517: Koopman Operator \( \mathcal{K \): The Koopman operator \( \mathcal{K : \mathcal{H \to \mathcal{H \) is defined by \( \mathcal{K \phi = \phi \circ f \), where \( f: \mathcal{X \to \mathcal{X \) represents the dynamical system's evolution map, and \( \phi \in \mathcal{H \).}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-518: Observable Subspace \( \mathcal{F_n \): Let \( \mathcal{F _n = \text{span}\{ \phi_1, \phi_2, \dots, \phi_n \} \subset \mathcal{H \) be a finite-dimensional subspace spanned by \( n \) observables \( \{ \phi_i \}_{i=1 ^n \).}}}}
elements.sent-2411-519: Projection Operator \( P_n \): \( P_n : \mathcal{H \to \mathcal{F _n \) denotes the orthogonal projection operator onto \( \mathcal{F _n \).}}}
elements.sent-2411-520: The Koopman operator $\mathcal{K}$ is an infinite-dimensional linear operator that acts on observable functions $\phi$ of the state space $\mathbb{R ^d$ as follows: \begin{equation}K \phi(x) = \phi(f(x))\end{equation} where $f: \mathbb{R ^d \rightarrow \mathbb{R ^d$ defines the dynamical system, and $x \in \mathbb{R ^d$ represents the state at a given time step.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-521: Eigenfunctions of the Koopman operator satisfy: \begin{equation}K \phi = \lambda \phi\end{equation} where $\lambda \in \mathbb{C}$ are the corresponding eigenvalues.
elements.sent-2411-522: These eigenfunctions encapsulate intrinsic properties of the dynamical system, such as growth rates and oscillatory modes.
elements.sent-2411-523: EDMD is a data-driven method to approximate the Koopman operator within a finite-dimensional subspace.
elements.sent-2411-524: Given a set of observables $\{\phi_1, \phi_2, \dots, \phi_n\}$, EDMD seeks a finite-dimensional Koopman matrix $K \in \mathbb{R ^{n \times n}$ that best satisfies: \begin{equation}\phi_i(f(x)) \approx \sum_{j=1}^n K_{ji} \phi_j(x), \quad \forall i = 1, \dots, n\end{equation} Formally, given data pairs $\{(x_t, x_{t+1 )\}_{t=1 ^m$, EDMD formulates the optimization problem: \begin{equation}\min_{K} \sum_{t=1}^m \left\| \Phi(x_{t+1}) - K \Phi(x_t) \right\|_2^2 \label{eq:k_edmd}\end{equation} where $\Phi(x) = [\phi_1(x), \phi_2(x), \dots, \phi_n(x)]^\top$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-525: Assume the Gram matrix $G$ is invertible, the closed-form solution is obtained by: \begin{equation}K = G^{-1} A G = \frac{1}{m} \sum_{t=1}^m \Phi(x_t) \Phi(x_t)^\top A = \frac{1}{m} \sum_{t=1}^m \Phi(x_t) \Phi(x_{t+1})^\top\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-526: Notably, the convergence of the EDMD algorithm has been rigorously established in previous works \cite{korda2018convergence,korda2018linear .}
elements.sent-2411-527: For example, \cite{korda2018convergence demonstrates that, under mild assumptions, as the sample size \( m \to \infty \) and the number of observables \( n \to \infty \), the estimated Koopman operator \( K \) converges to the true Koopman operator \( \mathcal{K \) within the EDMD framework.}}
elements.sent-2411-528: In contrast to previous works, our contribution lies in establishing statistical error bounds for the proposed algorithm, rather than merely proving convergence in the asymptotic regime of infinite samples and observables.
elements.sent-2411-529: To achieve this, we make the following assumptions:.
elements.sent-2411-531: The dynamical system \( x_{t+1 = f(x_t) \) is ergodic and stationary, ensuring that time averages converge to ensemble averages.}
elements.sent-2411-532: Each component of the observable function \( \Phi(x) = z \in \mathbb{R ^n \) is bounded.}
elements.sent-2411-533: Specifically, there exists a constant \( C > 0 \) such that for all \( i = 1, 2, \dots, n \) and for all \( x \in \mathbb{R ^d \), $ |\phi_i(x)| = |z_i| \leq C.. $.}
elements.sent-2411-535: The sequence of observable subspaces $\mathcal{F _n = \text{span}\{ \phi, \cdots, \phi_n \}$ is dense in $\mathcal{H}$ as $n \rightarrow \infty$.}
elements.sent-2411-536: Each observable \( \phi_i \) for \( i = 1, 2, \dots, n \) is a linearly independent basis function.
elements.sent-2411-537: This ensures that any observable in $\mathcal{H}$ can be approximated arbitrarily well by elements in $ \mathcal{F _n $ for sufficiently large $ n $.}
elements.sent-2411-538: Invertibility of Gram Matrix: The true gram matrix $ \mathbb{E [G]$ and estimated gram matrix $G$ are invertible with its smallest eigenvalue bounded below by $\gamma > 0$, such as $\lambda_{\min (\mathbb{E [G]) \geq \gamma, \lambda_{\min (G) \geq \gamma $.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-539: Based on Assumption 1, which pertains to ergodicity and stationarity, the sequence of iterates \( \{x_t\}_{t=1 ^m \) can be approximated as independently and identically distributed (i.i.d.) for a sufficiently large number of samples \cite{korda2018convergence .}}
elements.sent-2411-540: Assumption 2 can be fulfilled in neural network implementations by employing bounded activation functions (e.g., sigmoid) or by constraining the neural network weights.
elements.sent-2411-541: Assumption 3 is supported by the universal approximation theorem of the neural networks \cite{hornik1989multilayer , which asserts that neural network-based observables are capable of approximating Koopman operators as the observable dimension $n$ increases.}
elements.sent-2411-542: Finally, Assumption 4 is utilized to derive the closed-form solution for the Koopman operator, and it can be extended to the Moore–Penrose inverse to allow for some relaxation.. Theorem 1.
elements.sent-2411-543: Given the above four assumptions, the statistical error bound of the spectral norm between the estimated Koopman operator \( K \) and the true Koopman operator \( \mathcal{K \) is \begin{equation}\|K - K\| \leq O\left(\frac{1}{n^{\alpha}}\right) + O\left({\frac{n^2}{m}}\right),\end{equation} where \( m \) is the sample size, \( n \) is the dimension of the observable function (number of observables), and \( \alpha > 0 \) denotes the decay rate of the projection error, which is related to the original dynamics and observable functions.}
elements.sent-2411-545: For data samples \( m = \mathcal{O(n^3) \), the bound simplifies to \begin{equation}\|K - K\| \leq O\left(\frac{1}{n^{\alpha}}\right) + O\left(\frac{1}{n}\right).\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-546: Thus, for $ m =\omega(n^2)$, as \( n \to \infty \), we have \begin{equation}\lim_{m =\omega(n^2), \, n \rightarrow \infty} \|K - K\| = 0.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-547: In the following sections, we provide a detailed proof of Theorem 1, elaborating on the convergence rates and the interplay between sample size \( m \) and observable dimension \( n \).
elements.sent-2411-548: The total error in approximating the true Koopman operator \( \mathcal{K \) with its learned finite-dimensional estimate \( K \) can be decomposed into two components: projection error and sampling error.}
elements.sent-2411-549: Sampling error: The sampling error, $\epsilon_{\text{est (n, m) = \| K -\mathbb{E [K] \|$, originates from estimating the finite-dimensional Koopman operator \( K \) using a finite number of data samples \( m \).}}}
elements.sent-2411-550: It quantifies the discrepancy between the estimated operator and the true finite-dimensional operator, $\mathbb{E [K]$, due to the limited amount of data.}
elements.sent-2411-551: Projection error: The projection error, $\epsilon_{\text{proj (n) = \| \mathbb{E [K] - \mathcal{K \|$, arises from approximating the infinite-dimensional Koopman operator $\mathcal{K}$ within a finite-dimensional subspace spanned by a finite set of EDMD observables.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-552: Specifically, $\mathbb{E [K]$ approximates $\mathcal{K}$ in the finite-dimensional subspace $\mathcal{H _n = \text{span}\{\phi_1, \phi_2, \dots, \phi_n\}$, where $\{\phi_i\}$ are the selected observables.}}
elements.sent-2411-553: Then the total error between the estimated Koopman matrix $K$ and the true Koopman operator $\mathcal{K}$ is given by: \begin{equation}\| K - K \| \leq \| K - E[K] \| + \| E[K]- K \| \nonumber = \epsilon_{est}(n, m) + \epsilon_{proj}(n)\end{equation}.

elements.sent-2411-578: However, achieving accurate linearization through data-driven methods is difficult due to approximation error and domain shifts.
elements.sent-2411-579: This paper addresses these challenges by proposing an Incremental Koopman algorithm designed to iteratively refine Koopman dynamics for high-dimensional legged robots.
elements.sent-2411-580: The key idea is to progressively expand the dataset and latent space dimension, enabling the learned Koopman dynamics to converge towards true system dynamics.
elements.sent-2411-581: Experimental results demonstrate its superiority on robots like Unitree G1, H1, A1, Go2, and ANYmal D, across various terrains.
elements.sent-2411-582: This is the first work to apply linearized whole body dynamics with the Koopman Operator for locomotion control of high-dimensional legged robots, providing a scalable model-based control solution.
elements.sent-2411-583: The code can be found at: \url{https://github.com/intelligent-control-lab/Incremental-Koopman .}
elements.sent-2411-584: Control of legged robots has attracted growing interest, driven by the potential of humanoid and quadruped robots in real-world applications.
elements.sent-2411-585: However, the task remains challenging due to the high-dimensional, nonlinear dynamics of legged locomotion.
elements.sent-2411-586: Control strategies for nonlinear systems face a trade-off between controller complexity and modeling effort, broadly categorized as model-free or model-based.
elements.sent-2411-587: Local linearization methods, iLQR and NMPC often combine analytical \cite{sotaro2023analytical,zhu2024convergentilqrsafetrajectory,zhang2024robotsattitudesingularityfreequaternionbased or learned models \cite{nagabandi2017neuralnetworkdynamicsmodelbased, liu2023modelbasedcontrolsparseneural with complex controllers, requiring extensive domain expertise.}}
elements.sent-2411-588: A promising alternative to these approaches is employing Koopman Operator Theory to construct globally linearized models of legged systems and enabling the use of a simpler controller, e.g., linear MPC.
elements.sent-2411-589: While analytical approaches are extremely challenging for high-dimensional nonlinear systems like legged robots, our method adopts a data-driven approach that reduces reliance on domain expertise and enhances generalizability.
elements.sent-2411-590: To address these challenges, we propose an incremental Koopman algorithm for high-dimensional legged robots that continually learn and lift Koopman dynamics.
elements.sent-2411-591: Our approach gradually expands both the training dataset and the latent space dimension.
elements.sent-2411-592: The core idea is that this expansion progressively spans a state space where refined Koopman dynamics reduce linear approximation errors, ultimately converging to the true Koopman operator, as supported by our theoretical analysis.
elements.sent-2411-593: Experimental results demonstrate that our approach reduces linearization error within a few iterations and enables effective locomotion control for legged robots using Model Predictive Control (MPC).
elements.sent-2411-594: Our approach is the first work to implement locomotion control for high-dimensional legged robots using linearized whole body dynamics with Koopman Operator.
elements.sent-2411-595: Compared to traditional NMPC or RL mehods, our approach offers a scalable way to accurately model the dynamics of high-dimensional systems without requiring extensive expert knowledge, while maintaining generalizability across tasks and systems.

elements.sent-2411-623: Current research on Koopman theory of control can be categorized into three main directions:.
elements.sent-2411-624: Koopman Operator as a Feature Encoder: The Koopman operator has been applied in some methods to predict future states based on the current state \cite{han2023utility or vision inputs \cite{chen2024korol .}}
elements.sent-2411-625: Integrating Koopman Operators with RL: Other methods combine the Koopman latent dynamic space with traditional RL policies, either hierarchically \cite{kim2024learning or end-to-end \cite{lyu2023task .}}
elements.sent-2411-626: Linear Control in Koopman Latent Space: A promising direction applies traditional linear control methods, such as LQR or MPC, to the Koopman-linearized model, emphasizing interpretability and flexibility.
